questions.question: What types of signals do cells in the nervous system use to communicate?
questions.explanation: Neurons signal electrically (action potentials along the membrane) and chemically (neurotransmitters at synapses).

questions.question: Which two structures make up the central nervous system (CNS)?
questions.explanation: The CNS is the integrating/control center: the brain and spinal cord. Nerves and ganglia belong to the PNS.

questions.question: What is the primary composition of the peripheral nervous system (PNS)?
choices: Only the sensory receptors in the skin
Nerves that extend from the brain and spinal cord
The sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions exclusively
questions.explanation: The PNS is everything outside the CNS — the nerves (and ganglia) that link the CNS to the rest of the body.

questions.question: Somatic sensory fibers convey impulses to the central nervous system from which structures?
choices: Smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands
questions.explanation: "Somatic" = body wall. Somatic sensory fibers carry input from skin, skeletal muscles, and joints.

questions.question: Visceral sensory fibers convey impulses to the central nervous system from which structures?
questions.explanation: "Visceral" refers to internal organs, so visceral sensory fibers carry input from the viscera.

questions.question: The motor (efferent) division of the PNS transmits impulses from the CNS to which effector organs?
questions.explanation: Efferent = carries commands away from the CNS to effectors, which are muscles and glands.

questions.question: Which nervous system division provides conscious, voluntary control of skeletal muscles?
questions.explanation: The somatic nervous system gives voluntary control over skeletal muscle; the autonomic system is involuntary.

questions.question: The autonomic nervous system regulates which of the following?
choices: Smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands
questions.explanation: The ANS (involuntary) controls smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.

questions.question: Which functional subdivision of the autonomic nervous system mobilizes body systems during activity?
questions.explanation: The sympathetic division is the "fight-or-flight" system that mobilizes the body during activity or stress.

questions.question: Which functional subdivision of the autonomic nervous system promotes house-keeping functions during rest?
questions.explanation: The parasympathetic division is "rest-and-digest," conserving energy and running housekeeping functions.

questions.question: What proportion of the volume of the nervous system is made up by neuroglia?
questions.explanation: Neuroglia make up roughly half the volume of the nervous system.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of neuroglia?
choices: They are the structural units of the nervous system
They conduct impulses over long distances
questions.explanation: Unlike neurons, glia are not electrically excitable and do not conduct impulses; they support neurons.

questions.question: Which type of neuroglia is the most abundant, versatile, and highly branched in the CNS?
questions.explanation: Astrocytes are the most abundant, versatile, and highly branched glia in the CNS.

questions.question: Controlling the chemical environment around neurons is a primary function of which CNS glial cell?
questions.explanation: Astrocytes buffer the chemical environment (e.g., ions, neurotransmitters) around neurons.

questions.question: Which CNS glial cells are small, ovoid cells with thorny processes that monitor neurons and migrate toward injured ones?
questions.explanation: Microglia are small, thorny cells that monitor neuron health and migrate toward injury.

questions.question: What defensive function can microglial cells perform in the CNS?
choices: Beating cilia to circulate cerebrospinal fluid
Transforming to phagocytize microorganisms and neuronal debris
Controlling the chemical environment around neurons
questions.explanation: Microglia are the CNS's immune cells — they transform to phagocytize microbes and dead neuron debris.

questions.question: Which glial cells range in shape from squamous to columnar and line the central cavities of the brain and spinal cord?
questions.explanation: Ependymal cells line the ventricles and central canal, which contain cerebrospinal fluid.

questions.question: What is the function of the cilia on ependymal cells?
choices: To beat and circulate cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
To exchange nutrients between capillaries and neurons
questions.explanation: The beating cilia of ependymal cells help circulate cerebrospinal fluid through the CNS cavities.

questions.question: Which CNS glial cells have branched processes that wrap around nerve fibers to form insulating myelin sheaths?
questions.explanation: Oligodendrocytes myelinate axons in the CNS. (Schwann cells do this in the PNS.)

questions.question: Which PNS glial cells surround neuron cell bodies and function similarly to astrocytes in the CNS?
questions.explanation: Satellite cells surround PNS cell bodies (in ganglia) and, like astrocytes, help control the environment.

questions.question: Which PNS glial cells surround all peripheral nerve fibers and form myelin sheaths in thicker nerve fibers?
questions.explanation: Schwann cells wrap all PNS axons and myelinate the thicker ones — the PNS equivalent of oligodendrocytes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a special characteristic of neurons?
choices: They have a very short lifespan
They have extreme longevity, lasting a person's lifetime
They have a low metabolic rate
They do not require a continuous supply of oxygen
questions.explanation: Neurons have extreme longevity — they can function for a person's entire lifetime.

questions.question: With few exceptions, what does it mean that neurons are amitotic?
questions.explanation: Amitotic means they lose the ability to divide — most neurons cannot be replaced if lost.

questions.question: Because neurons have a high metabolic rate, they require a continuous supply of which two substances?
questions.explanation: Neurons need a constant supply of oxygen and glucose; even brief interruptions can kill them.

questions.question: What is the biosynthetic center of a neuron that synthesizes proteins, membranes, and chemicals?
questions.explanation: The cell body (soma) is the biosynthetic center, making proteins, membranes, and chemicals.

questions.question: What term describes clusters of neuron cell bodies located in the central nervous system (CNS)?
questions.explanation: In the CNS, clusters of cell bodies are nuclei. (In the PNS they are ganglia.)

questions.question: What term describes clusters of neuron cell bodies located in the peripheral nervous system (PNS)?
questions.explanation: In the PNS, clusters of cell bodies are called ganglia. (In the CNS they are nuclei.)

questions.question: Bundles of neuron processes (fibers) within the central nervous system are called:
questions.explanation: Bundles of fibers in the CNS are tracts. (In the PNS they are nerves.)

questions.question: Bundles of neuron processes (fibers) within the peripheral nervous system are called:
questions.explanation: Bundles of fibers in the PNS are nerves. (In the CNS they are tracts.)

questions.question: Which short, diffusely branched neuron processes act as the receptive (input) region of the cell?
questions.explanation: Dendrites are the short, branched input (receptive) region that receives signals.

questions.question: Dendrites convey incoming messages toward the cell body in the form of short distance signals known as:
questions.explanation: Dendrites carry short-distance graded potentials toward the cell body (axons carry action potentials).

questions.question: The single axon of a neuron typically starts at a cone-shaped area called the:
questions.explanation: The axon arises from the cone-shaped axon hillock on the cell body.

questions.question: Very long axons within the nervous system are specifically referred to as:
questions.explanation: Long axons are called nerve fibers.

questions.question: Occasional branches along the length of an axon are called:
questions.explanation: Branches given off along the length of an axon are axon collaterals.

questions.question: The distal endings of an axon, which number as many as 10,000 terminal branches, are called:
questions.explanation: The distal endings are axon terminals (the secretory region), sometimes 10,000 branches.

questions.question: The axon terminal is the secretory region that releases what substances into the extracellular space?
questions.explanation: Axon terminals release neurotransmitters into the synaptic (extracellular) space.

questions.question: What type of axonal transport moves molecules and organelles away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: Anterograde transport moves cargo away from the cell body toward the axon terminal.

questions.question: What type of axonal transport moves molecules and organelles toward the cell body?
questions.explanation: Retrograde transport moves cargo back toward the cell body.

questions.question: Certain viruses and bacterial toxins, such as polio, rabies, and tetanus toxin, damage neural tissues by utilizing which mechanism?
questions.explanation: Polio, rabies, and tetanus toxin hijack retrograde transport to travel back to the cell body.

questions.question: What is a primary function of the myelin sheath composed of a protein-lipid substance?
choices: To protect and electrically insulate the axon
questions.explanation: The myelin sheath protects and electrically insulates the axon.

questions.question: How does the presence of a myelin sheath affect nerve impulse transmission?
choices: It decreases the speed of transmission
It reverses the direction of transmission
It increases the speed of nerve impulse transmission
It randomly stops nerve impulse transmission
It has no effect on transmission speed
questions.explanation: Myelin speeds transmission by allowing the impulse to jump between nodes (saltatory conduction).

questions.question: The gaps between adjacent Schwann cells, where axon collaterals can emerge, were formerly called:
questions.explanation: The myelin gaps between Schwann cells are the nodes of Ranvier (myelin sheath gaps).

questions.question: How many axons can a single oligodendrocyte wrap its processes around at once in the CNS?
questions.explanation: One oligodendrocyte can myelinate up to about 60 axons at once — unlike a Schwann cell, which myelinates just one.

questions.question: Regions of the brain and spinal cord with dense collections of myelinated fibers are referred to as:
questions.explanation: Myelin is whitish, so densely myelinated fiber regions are white matter.

questions.question: Gray matter in the nervous system is mostly composed of:
choices: Neuron cell bodies and nonmyelinated fibers
questions.explanation: Gray matter is mostly neuron cell bodies and unmyelinated fibers.

questions.question: Which structural classification describes a neuron with three or more processes (one axon and multiple dendrites)?
questions.explanation: Multipolar neurons have one axon and many dendrites (three or more processes total).

questions.question: What is the most common and major neuron type in the central nervous system?
questions.explanation: Multipolar neurons are the most common type in the CNS.

questions.question: Bipolar neurons, which possess exactly two processes, are rare but can be found in which specific locations?
questions.explanation: Rare bipolar neurons are found in special sense organs — the retina and olfactory mucosa.

questions.question: Which neurons are grouped functionally as carrying sensory information from receptors to the CNS?
questions.explanation: Sensory (afferent) neurons carry input from receptors toward the CNS. (Afferent = toward.)

questions.question: Which neurons connect sensory and motor neurons, and are located entirely within the CNS?
questions.explanation: Interneurons (association neurons) lie entirely within the CNS and link sensory to motor neurons.

quizzes.title: Slide Deck 2 — Practice Quiz
quizzes.sub: The brain · cerebrum, diencephalon, brain stem & cerebellum

questions.question: What is the approximate mass of an average human brain?
questions.explanation: The average adult brain weighs about 1300 g — almost 3 pounds.

questions.question: Approximately how many neurons are found in the human brain?
questions.explanation: The brain contains roughly 85 billion neurons.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four main regions of the adult human brain?
questions.explanation: The four regions are the cerebral hemispheres, diencephalon, brain stem, and cerebellum. The spinal cord is part of the CNS but not a region of the brain.

questions.question: Which three structures make up the brain stem?
choices: Basal nuclei, cerebral cortex, and white matter
questions.explanation: The brain stem consists of the midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata.

questions.question: What percentage of total brain mass is accounted for by the cerebrum?
questions.explanation: The cerebrum makes up about 83% of total brain mass.

questions.question: The surface of the cerebral cortex contains folds that roll upon themselves. These folds are called:
questions.explanation: The elevated ridges (folds) of cortical tissue are gyri.

questions.question: Shallow grooves on the surface of the cerebrum are referred to as:
questions.explanation: Shallow grooves are sulci; the ridges between them are gyri.

questions.question: Deep grooves on the surface of the cerebrum are referred to as:
questions.explanation: The deeper grooves are fissures (shallow grooves are sulci).

questions.question: Which deep groove separates the cerebrum into right and left cerebral hemispheres?
questions.explanation: The longitudinal fissure separates the two cerebral hemispheres.

questions.question: Which groove separates the frontal lobe from the parietal lobe?
questions.explanation: The central sulcus divides the frontal lobe from the parietal lobe.

questions.question: Which fissure separates the cerebrum from the cerebellum?
questions.explanation: The transverse cerebral fissure separates the cerebrum from the cerebellum below it.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five lobes of a cerebral hemisphere?
questions.explanation: The five lobes are frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, and insula. 'Sphenoid' is a bone, not a lobe.

questions.question: Which lobe of the cerebrum is buried under portions of the temporal, parietal, and frontal lobes?
questions.explanation: The insula lies deep within the lateral sulcus, buried by the surrounding lobes.

questions.question: The lateral sulcus primarily outlines which lobe of the cerebrum?
questions.explanation: The lateral sulcus outlines the temporal lobe.

questions.question: Each cerebral hemisphere consists of three basic regions. Which of the following is NOT one of those regions?
choices: Superficial cerebral cortex of gray matter
Basal nuclei deep within the white matter
None of the above (A, B, and C are the three regions)
questions.explanation: The three regions are the cerebral cortex, internal white matter, and basal nuclei. The cerebellar cortex belongs to the cerebellum, not the cerebral hemispheres.

questions.question: What is the approximate thickness of the cerebral cortex?
questions.explanation: The cerebral cortex is only about 2–4 mm thick.

questions.question: The cerebral cortex accounts for approximately what percentage of the total mass of the brain?
questions.explanation: Despite being thin, the folded cortex accounts for about 40% of total brain mass.

questions.question: Which type of functional area in the cerebral cortex is responsible for the conscious awareness of sensation?
questions.explanation: Sensory areas provide conscious awareness of sensation.

questions.question: Which type of functional area in the cerebral cortex integrates diverse information?
questions.explanation: Association areas integrate diverse information to enable purposeful action.

questions.question: Each cerebral hemisphere is primarily concerned with the sensory and motor functions of the opposite side of the body. This is known as being:
questions.explanation: Each hemisphere serves the opposite (contralateral) side of the body.

questions.question: In which lobe of the cerebrum are the motor areas located?
questions.explanation: The motor areas lie in the frontal lobe.

questions.question: The primary (somatic) motor cortex is located in which specific convolution?
questions.explanation: The primary motor cortex occupies the precentral gyrus of the frontal lobe.

questions.question: Large neurons in the primary motor cortex that allow conscious control of precise, skilled, skeletal muscle movements are called:
questions.explanation: The large pyramidal cells give rise to the pyramidal (corticospinal) tracts controlling skilled movement.

questions.question: Which motor area is located anterior to the precentral gyrus and helps plan movements or acts as a staging area for skilled motor activities?
questions.explanation: The premotor cortex, just anterior to the precentral gyrus, plans and stages skilled movements.

questions.question: Which motor area is usually present in only one hemisphere (typically the left) and directs the muscles of speech production?
questions.explanation: Broca's area, usually in the left hemisphere, directs the muscles used in speech production.

questions.question: Damage to areas of the primary motor cortex, such as from a stroke, typically causes what symptom?
choices: Paralysis of voluntary muscles on the opposite side of the body
Paralysis of voluntary muscles on the same side of the body
questions.explanation: Because motor control is contralateral, damage causes paralysis on the opposite side of the body.

questions.question: The primary somatosensory cortex is located in which region?
choices: Precentral gyrus of the frontal lobe
Postcentral gyri of the parietal lobe
Superior margin of the temporal lobe
Posterior tip of the occipital lobe
questions.explanation: The primary somatosensory cortex sits in the postcentral gyri of the parietal lobe.

questions.question: The primary somatosensory cortex receives general sensory information from the skin and proprioceptors of all the following EXCEPT:
choices: Smooth muscle in the digestive tract
questions.explanation: It receives somatic (body-wall) input — skin, skeletal muscles, joints, tendons — not sensation from smooth muscle of the viscera.

questions.question: Which functional area is located posterior to the primary somatosensory cortex and integrates sensory input to determine the size, texture, and relationship of parts of objects being felt?
questions.explanation: The somatosensory association cortex integrates touch input to identify objects by feel (stereognosis).

questions.question: The primary visual cortex is located in which lobe of the brain?
questions.explanation: The primary visual cortex is in the occipital lobe.

questions.question: A patient can see visual stimuli but cannot comprehend or recognize what they are looking at (e.g., they cannot recognize faces). Which area of the brain is most likely damaged?
questions.explanation: The visual association area interprets visual stimuli; damage leaves sight intact but impairs recognition.

questions.question: The primary auditory cortex is located in the superior margin of which lobe?
questions.explanation: The primary auditory cortex lies along the superior margin of the temporal lobe.

questions.question: Which sensory area is responsible for the conscious awareness of balance and the position of the head in space?
questions.explanation: The vestibular cortex provides conscious awareness of balance and head position.

questions.question: The primary olfactory cortex is involved in the conscious awareness of:
questions.explanation: The olfactory cortex handles the conscious awareness of smell.

questions.question: The gustatory cortex is involved in the perception of taste. Where is it located?
questions.explanation: The gustatory (taste) cortex is located in the insula.

questions.question: Conscious perception of visceral sensations, such as an upset stomach or a full bladder, is processed in the:
questions.explanation: The visceral sensory area gives conscious perception of visceral sensations.

questions.question: Which structure acts as a major relay station for most sensory impulses that reach the cerebral cortex from the spinal cord and brain stem?
questions.explanation: The thalamus is the major relay station, editing and sorting sensory input on its way to the cortex.

questions.question: The epithalamus contains the pineal gland. What is the primary function of the melatonin secreted by this gland?
questions.explanation: Melatonin from the pineal gland helps regulate the sleep-wake (circadian) cycle.

questions.question: Which diencephalon structure acts as the autonomic control center, regulating blood pressure, heart rate, digestive tract, and respiration rate?
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus is the main autonomic control center.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the hypothalamus?
choices: Regulation of hunger and water balance
Control of the endocrine system via the pituitary gland
Control center for emotions and basic drives
questions.explanation: Conscious visual perception is a cortex function; the hypothalamus handles temperature, hunger/thirst, endocrine control, and emotions.

questions.question: Which region of the brain stem is located between the diencephalon and the pons?
questions.explanation: The midbrain lies between the diencephalon above and the pons below.

questions.question: Nuclei in the brain stem are associated with how many of the 12 pairs of cranial nerves?
questions.explanation: Brain stem nuclei are associated with 10 of the 12 pairs of cranial nerves.

questions.question: Which region of the brain stem conveys motor impulses from the cerebrum to the cerebellum and regulates auditory and visual reflexes?
questions.explanation: The midbrain relays cerebrum-to-cerebellum motor impulses and houses auditory/visual reflex centers (corpora quadrigemina).

questions.question: Which region of the brain stem is composed of conduction tracts, connects higher brain centers to the spinal cord, and contains pneumotaxic and apneustic areas for respiration control?
questions.explanation: The pons contains conduction tracts and the pneumotaxic/apneustic respiratory centers.

questions.question: The medulla oblongata blends into the spinal cord at which anatomical landmark?
questions.explanation: The medulla blends into the spinal cord at the foramen magnum of the skull.

questions.question: Which fluid-filled cavity is contained within the medulla oblongata?
questions.explanation: The fourth ventricle lies within the pons and medulla.

questions.question: The cardiovascular center, which adjusts the force and rate of heart contraction and blood vessel diameter, is located in the:
questions.explanation: The cardiovascular center is in the medulla oblongata.

questions.question: Which of the following autonomic functions is NOT regulated by the various centers of the medulla oblongata?
questions.explanation: The medulla controls vomiting, swallowing, hiccupping, coughing, and sneezing; thermoregulation is a hypothalamic function.

questions.question: Which part of the brain processes input from the cortex, brain stem, and sensory receptors to provide precise, coordinated movements of skeletal muscles?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates and fine-tunes skeletal muscle movement.

questions.question: The cerebellum occupies the inferior and posterior aspects of the cranial cavity and consists of two hemispheres separated by a central structure called the:
questions.explanation: The vermis is the wormlike central structure connecting the two cerebellar hemispheres.
